preamble: The transformation of a velar nasal (ŋ) into an alveolar lateral approximant (l) involves a significant shift in both the place and manner of articulation. This sound change entails the loss of nasality (denasalization), a move from a velar (back of the mouth) to an alveolar (ridge behind teeth) place of articulation, and a change from a nasal stop to a lateral approximant. While not a universally common shift, it is attested in specific linguistic contexts, demonstrating the diverse pathways of phonetic evolution.
phoneticEffects: Denasalization, Lateralization, Alveolarization, Manner Shift (Nasal to Approximant), Place Shift (Velar to Alveolar)

examples.note: The nominal marker /li/ in the Yichun language is argued to be the result of a historical sound change from an earlier velar nasal (ŋ).

sources: Li, Xuping (2018). A Grammar of Gan Chinese: The Yichun Language. Berlin: De Gruyter Mouton.
